mnemonics.title: CUDA Memory Transfer Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: H2D = Host to Device (cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyHostToDevice), D2H = Device to Host. Think: 'H to D, write to device; D to H, harvest results' - always copy inputs H2D, compute on GPU, copy results D2H.

mnemonics.title: Thread Index Formula
mnemonics.content: GLOBAL INDEX = blockIdx × blockDim + threadIdx. Remember: 'Big Block times Dim plus Tiny Thread' - blockIdx (big groups) determines base offset, threadIdx (within block) adds local offset.

mnemonics.title: Kernel Launch Syntax
mnemonics.content: kernel_name<<<gridDim, blockDim>>>(args). Think: 'Grid groups blocks, Block holds threads' - first param is number of blocks (grid), second is threads per block.

acronyms.usage: NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and API for GPU programming.
Execution model where one instruction operates on multiple data elements simultaneously - how GPUs process vector addition.
The GPU processing unit that executes warps of threads. Vector addition distributes work across multiple SMs.
Bus used for data transfer between host CPU and GPU device. Bandwidth limit affects vector addition performance for large vectors.
The device that executes parallel CUDA kernels for vector addition.
Direction of cudaMemcpy for copying input vectors a and b from CPU to GPU memory.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 2D grid of Post-it notes: Grid = entire bulletin board, each Block = a group of Post-its (thread cluster), each Post-it = one thread. Each thread knows its Block position (blockIdx) and position within Block (threadIdx), giving unique global index for array element.

Think of 32 swimmers (warp) diving in sequence into consecutive lanes (memory addresses). They dive together (SIMD) and emerge together - efficient! Random access is like swimmers diving into random lanes one-by-one - very inefficient.

Draw two separate rooms: Host (CPU) Room has original arrays a,b and empty result c. Device (GPU) Room has copies. Operations: (1) Copy a,b to GPU Room, (2) Workers in GPU Room do addition, (3) Copy result c back to Host Room. Rooms don't share addresses!

analogies.concept: Vector Addition as Factory Assembly Line
analogies.analogy: Imagine a factory assembly line: Input array A has parts, array B has other parts. Each GPU thread is a worker station. Worker 0 assembles part 0 from A[0]+B[0], Worker 1 does [1], etc. All work simultaneously. With stride pattern, after completing assigned tasks, workers check for more work - efficient load balancing.

analogies.concept: Grid and Block as Organization Hierarchy
analogies.analogy: Think of grading 10,000 exam papers: Each Block = one teaching assistant (TA), each TA manages BlockDim student graders (threads). blockIdx tells which TA, threadIdx tells which grader. Global index = TA number × students_per_TA + grader_number. Multiple TAs work in parallel, each TA's graders work in parallel.

cudaMalloc is like reserving hotel rooms on the GPU 'Resort' (device). You get room numbers (pointers) but can't stay there yourself - only GPU workers can use those rooms. cudaMemcpy is the shuttle bus输送 guests (data) to/from the resort. cudaFree is checking out. Don't forget to checkout or you'll have memory leaks!
